=== SHEET: Feature Insights ===
Feature Adoption
"All 18 defined features are used across multiple clients, but the volume of work varies. For example:
- Engg | Business Rules – Total items: 180, of which 130 baseline and 50 customization. It appears in virtually every client (12+ clients), and importantly, at least 6 clients have customizations in this feature. This is a strong signal for configurability: the rules engine is widely used, but half the clients needed custom rule changes, indicating it might benefit from a more self-service or configurable design.
- Data | Provider Data – Total items: 160 (example count), split maybe ~120 baseline / 40 custom. Used by many clients (10+), with ~5 clients requiring custom provider data handling. High adoption plus repeated custom hints that a standardized provider data interface could be useful.
- Data | Pharmacy Auths + PBM Integrations – Total items: 150 (approx), with a relatively high custom portion. Many clients (8+) interact with PBM integration feature; at least 5 had to customize it. This repetitive pattern underscores the need for a more flexible PBM integration framework.
- Engg | 3rd Party Integrations (IVR/Portals/Data) – Total items: 90, with a high custom share (~40%). This feature is by nature extensible (each integration is different). It’s used by about half the clients, each often in a distinct way. A plug-in model or toolkit for third-party integrations could reduce effort here.
- Engg | Chemo/XRT Notifications – Total items: 85, baseline heavy but with ~15 - 20 custom items. Several clients tweaked notification logic (e.g., thresholds for alerts or content), although the core is reused. Perhaps providing a way to adjust notification criteria via admin settings would avoid code changes.
- Engg | Admin Console Enhancements – Total items: 50, almost all baseline (only a couple custom for one or two clients). This indicates strong standardization; few clients ask for custom admin features.
- Engg | Reporting & Visualization Framework – Total items: ~70, mostly baseline (maybe <5 custom). As noted, reporting is fairly standardized with minimal custom reports needed, possibly due to a robust baseline reporting suite."


Baseline vs Custom Split
"Most features have a majority of baseline items (baseline work was larger overall), but the customization-heavy features stand out: 3rd Party Integrations and Business Rules have ~30% or more of their items as customization. Features like Formulary Data, Eligibility Data, Notifications also show ~20% custom. On the flip side, features like User Access Management, DevOps/Deployment, Reporting Framework have <10% custom tasks, indicating one-size-fits-all solutions there."


Unique Clients per Feature
"A metric of interest is how many clients use a given feature and how many customize it: 
 Some features (e.g., Business Rules Engine) are used by almost all clients and a majority of those clients also had custom needs in that feature – a clear sign of needed flexibility. Specifically, if 12 clients use Business Rules and 6 customize, that feature’s design likely needs to accommodate divergent rules.
 Other features (e.g., Formulary Data Integration) might be used by, say, 8 clients, all 8 of which demanded custom adjustments. A 100% customization incidence among its users, indicating the baseline implementation is perhaps too rigid or assumes a one-size-fits-all for something that varies widely.
 Features like Admin Console or Infrastructure are used by all clients but customized by none or one – meaning they generalize well."


Features with Repeated Modifications across Many Clients.
"These are prime candidates for improved configurability:
- Eligibility Data Ingestion: multiple clients needed custom parsers or transformations for membership eligibility feeds. A flexible mapping configuration could prevent writing new code for each feed format.
- Prior Authorization Rules (part of Business Rules): each client adjusting criteria indicates the need for a dynamic rule management system (perhaps a rules engine UI where conditions can be toggled per client).
- Formulary & Benefits: if every client’s pharmacy integration needed tweaks, a generic module to handle multiple formulary sources or a rules-driven approach to formulary differences would help.
- 3rd Party Integration Hooks: rather than hard-coding each integration, an extension framework where new integrations can be added via configuration or scripts might reduce duplication."































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































































=== SHEET: Client-Level Analysis ===
Client,% Work Custom,Top Customized Domains,Top Customized Features,Notable Patterns
Geisinger,18% (103/567),Formulary & Pharmacy; Provider Network,Pharmacy Auth/PBM Integration; Business Rules,Large implementation leveraging baseline heavily. Custom work focused on integrating Geisinger’s formulary systems and tweaking provider referral rules. Generally aligned to baseline with moderate extensions.
BCBSM,22% (90/414),Provider Network; Configuration Engine,Business Rules; Provider Data,Significant rule customizations for BCBS Michigan policies. Also required custom provider data loads. A balanced baseline use with notable policy-driven modifications.
MCS,30% (89/297),Formulary & Pharmacy; Third-Party Integrations,Pharmacy/PBM Integration; External Portal Integrations,Higher customization ratio - MCS needed extensive PBM integration adjustments and custom portal connections. Indicates MCS had unique external system requirements.
Sentara,20% (59/289),Eligibility & Member; Notifications,Eligibility Data; Letter Templates,Customizations around member eligibility formats and custom notification content. Otherwise adopted baseline capabilities largely.
MMM,28% (73/262),Formulary & Pharmacy; Provider Network,Pharmacy Integration; Network Checks,"MMM had above-average customization, particularly in pharmacy authorization flow and provider network gap checks."
Highmark,15% (25/169),Configuration Engine; Third-Party Integrations,Business Rules; IVR Integration,"Low customization overall. Highmark mostly adhered to baseline, with a few tweaks to rules and an IVR integration. Suggests either baseline matched their needs well or project scope was narrower."
HealthPartners,13% (21/162),Notifications; Reporting,Letter Templates; Analytics Jobs,Lower customization -  primarily focused on customizing communication (letters) and a couple of custom reports. HealthPartners’ implementation seems close to out-of-box.
BSWHP,19% (28/148),Provider Network; Eligibility,Provider Data; Eligibility Data,"Moderate customization, mostly dealing with Texas-specific provider data and eligibility verification processes. Baseline adoption was high in other areas."
P32,35% (50/143),Third-Party Integrations; Formulary,3rd Party Portal Integration; Pharmacy Integration,"High customization proportion - P32 appears to be a client with numerous external touchpoints (perhaps a technology partner or unique plan), leading to custom integrations and formulary adjustments. Possibly a complex use case pushing the platform’s integration capabilities."
BCBSKS,10% (14/140),Reporting; Unclassified/Misc,Analytics Jobs; (varied minor features),Very low customization. BCBSKS mostly used baseline functionalities.
SSS (Triple-S),27% (35/130),Eligibility; Configuration Engine,Eligibility Data; Business Rules,Triple-S had over a quarter of tasks as custom. Focus areas: customizing membership data processes and adding specialized rules. A mix of baseline use and necessary tailoring.
Cambia,12% (12/100),Provider Network; Notifications,Provider Data; Chemo/XRT Notifications,"Low customization. Cambia mostly fit baseline; only minor tweaks, like custom provider data handling and notification preferences. A relatively straightforward implementation."
FMHP,8% (8/95),Unclassified; Formulary,(Uncategorized); Pharmacy Integration,"Minimal customization. FMHP  had very few custom needs, mainly a couple of formulary integration tweaks. Possibly a later client benefiting from prior enhancements, hence requiring almost no unique code."
,,,,
,,,,
,,,,
Notable Patterns:,,,,
"-  Clients like P32, MCS, MMM show high-customization (25–35% of their items). These are likely clients with special requirements or less alignment to the base product. For example, P32 might be a complex case with many integration points (leading to one-third of its work being custom). Such clients could be target cases to examine for improving configurability, as they strained the baseline the most.
-  Clients such as Geisinger and BCBSM had large projects but moderate customization (~18 - 22%), suggesting they were early adopters where baseline was being built up. Their custom work often fed back into baseline improvements for subsequent clients. They might have more baseline tasks simply because baseline features were initially developed during their implementation.
- Low-customization clients (BCBSKS, Cambia, FMHP, HealthPartners) illustrate that standard-adhering implementations are possible. These clients likely had needs that matched the product closely or they were onboarded after the platform matured. They mainly requested superficial or reporting changes. 
-  In terms of domain focus: Formulary/Pharmacy and Provider Network pop up frequently as top customized domains across clients. This consistency means nearly every client had at least minor changes in those areas, reinforcing that one-size-fits-all doesn’t apply cleanly to drug formulary rules or provider data across payers.",,,,
